did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses an ensemble deep learning approach for multi-class skin cancer classification. Specifically, it employs Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) including InceptionV3, Xception, and DenseNet201 as feature extractors, combined with a Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifier, to classify dermoscopic images of Basal Cell Carcinoma, Benign Keratosis, Melanocytic Nevus, and Melanoma using the ISIC2018 and ISIC2019 datasets.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is explicitly labeled as an 'Original Scientific Paper' in the title section. It presents original experimental work involving the development and evaluation of an ensemble deep learning model (combining CNNs and SVM) for multi-class skin cancer classification. The study details specific methodology (datasets ISIC2018/2019, data augmentation, model architecture), reports original results (accuracy of 94.46%), and compares these findings with existing methods, which characterizes it as a primary research article rather than a review.

title: Ensemble Deep Learning Approach For Multi-Class Skin Cancer Classification
journal: International Journal of Electrical and Computer Engineering Systems
brief_summary: This study developed an automated deep learning model for multi-class skin cancer classification, specifically targeting Basal Cell Carcinoma, Benign Keratosis, Melanocytic Nevus, and Melanoma. The researchers evaluated seven Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) using the ISIC2018 and ISIC2019 datasets, applying data augmentation to address class imbalance. InceptionV3, Xception, and DenseNet201 were identified as the top-performing individual networks and were utilized as feature extractors in an ensemble framework. These extracted features were concatenated and classified using a Support Vector Machine (SVM) algorithm. The proposed ensemble model achieved a classification accuracy of 94.46%, outperforming individual CNNs and existing ensemble methods like Max Voting. The results demonstrate the potential of combining CNN feature extraction with SVM classification to enhance the reliability of automated skin cancer diagnosis.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the four common classes used in the multi-class classification experiment after excluding rare classes from the ISIC2018 dataset.

all_task_types.target: Four-class skin cancer diagnosis (Basal Cell Carcinoma, Benign Keratosis Lesion, Melanocytic Nevus, and Melanoma)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multi-class classification of dermoscopic images into four discrete diagnostic categories. The model uses CNNs as feature extractors and an SVM classifier to predict class labels. Evaluation metrics include accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and AUC, confirming a classification task.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: The International Skin Imaging Collaboration 2018 dataset, containing dermoscopic images of skin lesions. The original dataset contained seven classes but was modified by excluding rare classes (VASC, DF, AKIEK) to focus on four common classes.

use.training: Used to train seven CNN models (GoogLeNet, InceptionV3, Xception, ResNet18, ResNet50, ResNet101, DenseNet201) with softmax classifiers to identify top-performing networks.
use.validation: Used for validation during the initial training phase (193 images).
use.testing: Used as the official test set for evaluation (1512 images originally, reduced to 1390 images after excluding rare classes).
use.other: Images from ISIC2019 were imported into ISIC2018 to create the combined dataset, ensuring no duplicates.

datasets_used.name: ISIC-2019
datasets_used.description: The International Skin Imaging Collaboration 2019 dataset. Images from this dataset were imported into the ISIC2018 dataset to address class imbalance, with duplicates removed.

use.training: Images were imported into the ISIC2018 dataset to create the 'ISIC2018+' combined dataset for training.
use.other: Used to augment the ISIC2018 dataset to balance class distribution.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A combined dataset created by merging ISIC2018 and non-duplicate images from ISIC2019, with rare classes (VASC, DF, AKIEK) excluded. It contains 16,787 images before augmentation.

use.training: Used as the primary dataset for training the top-performing CNNs (InceptionV3, Xception, DenseNet201) and the ensemble model.
use.other: Served as the base for the augmented dataset.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: The ISIC2018+ dataset after applying data augmentation techniques (scaling, flipping, rotation) to balance the classes. It contains 26,819 images.

use.training: Used to train the top-performing CNNs (InceptionV3, Xception, DenseNet201) individually to avoid overfitting and enhance generalization.
use.other: Used to mitigate overfitting during the training of the selected CNN feature extractors.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: No hypothesis testing was reported in the paper.

ISIC2018+.description: Evaluation of seven CNN models with softmax classifiers on the ISIC2018+ dataset (16,787 images).
ISIC2018+.notes: InceptionV3, Xception, and DenseNet201 achieved the highest accuracy among the seven models.

ISIC2018+(augmented).description: Evaluation of the top-performing models from the previous step trained individually on the augmented dataset (26,819 images).
ISIC2018+(augmented).notes: The ISIC2018+(augmented) dataset improved the performance of InceptionV3 but did not yield similar enhancements for Xception and DenseNet201 compared to their performance on ISIC2018+.

Ensemble Model (CNNs + SVM).description: The proposed ensemble model combining features from InceptionV3, Xception, and DenseNet201 classified via SVM.
Ensemble Model (CNNs + SVM).notes: The ensemble model outperformed individual CNNs (93.27%) and existing ensemble methods such as Max Voting (94.12%) and hybrid models like DenseNet201 with Random Forest (91.28%).

architecture_used.main_model: Ensemble of Convolutional Neural Networks (InceptionV3, Xception, DenseNet201) combined with a Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifier
architecture_used.pretraining: Transfer learning using pre-trained weights for GoogLeNet, InceptionV3, Xception, ResNet18, ResNet50, ResNet101, and DenseNet201
architecture_used.input_size: 224x224 pixels (for GoogLeNet, ResNet18, ResNet50, ResNet101, DenseNet201); 299x299 pixels (for InceptionV3, Xception)

architecture_details.fusion_method: Feature-level fusion by concatenating high-dimensional deep features extracted from the dense layers of the three CNNs without dimensionality reduction
architecture_details.svm_details: Used for multi-class classification via one-vs-all approach; optimized using Lagrange Multipliers to maximize margin

training_details.environment: MATLAB Deep Learning Toolbox (version 14.3) and Statistics and Machine Learning Toolbox (version 12.2)
training_details.hardware: Dell Precision 7740 laptop with NVIDIA Quadro RTX 5000 (16 GB GDDR6 VRAM)
training_details.learning_rate_schedule: Scheduled decay starting at 0.01, decaying by a factor of 1/10 every 10 epochs
training_details.data_augmentation: Image scaling (1 to 1.2), flipping (x-axis and y-axis), rotation (-90 to 90 degrees)

classification_or_output_head.cnn_head_modification: Original dense layer replaced with a dense layer having four outputs; classification layer replaced to output four class probabilities
classification_or_output_head.ensemble_head: SVM classifier taking concatenated feature vectors from the dense layers of InceptionV3, Xception, and DenseNet201 as input

preprocessing_techniques: Image resizing to adapt dermoscopic images to required CNN input dimensions (e.g., 224x224 pixels for GoogLeNet, ResNet18, ResNet50, ResNet101, DenseNet201; 299x299 pixels for InceptionV3, Xception)
Pixel intensity normalization converting values from [0, 255] range to [0, 1] range to prevent exploding gradients and stabilize training
Data augmentation including image scaling (1 to 1.2), flipping (x-axis and y-axis), and rotation (-90 to 90 degrees) to address class imbalance
Exclusion of rare lesion classes (Vascular Lesion, Dermatofibroma, Actinic Keratoses and Intraepithelial Carcinoma) from the dataset

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Scaling factor range of 1 to 1.2 applied with random parameters.
Flipping along the x-axis and y-axis with random parameters.
Rotation range from -90 to 90 degrees with random parameters.

Augmentations applied to underrepresented classes to achieve an equal distribution of 25% for each of the four target classes (BCC, BKL, NV, MEL), increasing the dataset size from 16,787 to 26,819 images.

conclusions: The proposed ensemble deep learning model, which combines features from InceptionV3, Xception, and DenseNet201 with a Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifier, achieved a classification accuracy of 94.46% for multi-class skin cancer diagnosis.

The proposed ensemble model outperformed individual top-performing CNNs (which achieved 93.27% accuracy) and existing ensemble methods such as Max Voting (94.12%) and hybrid models like DenseNet201 with Random Forest (91.28%).

Among seven evaluated CNN architectures (GoogLeNet, InceptionV3, Xception, ResNet18, ResNet50, ResNet101, and DenseNet201), InceptionV3, Xception, and DenseNet201 were identified as the top-performing networks for feature extraction.

The study demonstrates the potential of combining CNNs as feature extractors with SVM classifiers in an ensemble framework to enhance the accuracy and reliability of automated skin cancer classification.

Data augmentation techniques (scaling, flipping, rotation) and the integration of images from the ISIC2019 dataset into ISIC2018 were effective in addressing class imbalance, resulting in an equal distribution of 25% for each of the four target classes (BCC, BKL, NV, MEL) in the augmented dataset.

A distinctive feature-level fusion method that directly concatenates high-dimensional deep features from diverse CNN architectures without dimensionality reduction was utilized to preserve complete feature information for classification.

The SVM classifier, optimized using Lagrange Multipliers to maximize the margin, provided a computationally efficient alternative to traditional voting-based ensemble methods or prediction score fusion approaches.

The study excluded rare skin lesion types (Vascular Lesion, Dermatofibroma, and Actinic Keratoses and Intraepithelial Carcinoma) from the dataset to increase diagnostic performance on common types, acknowledging that high visual similarity between different skin lesions remains a challenge for achieving 100% accuracy.
important_remarks: The study excludes rare but clinically significant lesion classes (Vascular Lesion, Dermatofibroma, and Actinic Keratoses and Intraepithelial Carcinoma) to focus on four common classes, limiting the model's applicability to the full spectrum of skin lesions.
The dataset lacks diversity in terms of skin tones and age groups, which may introduce demographic biases and reduce the generalizability of the model to broader populations.

The model was trained exclusively on dermoscopic images captured under controlled conditions with specific lighting; its performance may degrade when applied to real-world data captured under varying conditions.
Data augmentation was used to artificially balance classes (25% each), which may not reflect true clinical prevalence and could impact the model's calibration in real-world settings.

The comparison with prior works is not strictly one-to-one, as some referenced studies did not utilize the official ISIC test set or performed classification on different numbers of classes (two, seven, or eight), making direct performance comparisons difficult.
The proposed ensemble model exhibits notable computational complexity, although it is claimed to be deployable in modest computing environments within dermatology clinics.
The paper does not report statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) for the performance differences between the proposed model and baseline methods.
There is no mention of external validation on independent datasets outside of the ISIC archive, raising concerns about overfitting to the specific distribution of the ISIC2018/2019 datasets.

dataset_usage_details.summary: The study utilized the ISIC2018 dataset, merging it with non-duplicate images from the ISIC2019 dataset to create a combined 'ISIC2018+' dataset. Rare classes (VASC, DF, AKIEK) were excluded, focusing on four classes: BCC, BKL, NV, and MEL. Data augmentation was applied to the training portion to balance class distribution (25% each). The model was trained on the augmented dataset and evaluated on the official ISIC test set. No external cross-dataset validation was performed; all experiments were confined to the ISIC archive.
dataset_usage_details.cross_dataset_validation_details: The paper does not report training on one named dataset and testing on a different unseen named dataset. The ISIC2019 images were merged into the ISIC2018 training pool to address imbalance, but the final evaluation was performed on the official ISIC2018 test set. There is no external holdout dataset from a different source or cohort used for final testing.

training.notes: Created by merging ISIC2018 and non-duplicate ISIC2019 images, excluding rare classes (VASC, DF, AKIEK). Used to train seven CNNs to identify top performers.

The ISIC2018+ dataset after applying scaling, flipping, and rotation to balance classes to 25% each. Used to train the top-performing CNNs (InceptionV3, Xception, DenseNet201) individually for the ensemble feature extraction.

validation.notes: Part of the original ISIC2018 split. The paper notes that validation and test sets remained unchanged after excluding rare categories. It is unclear if this specific subset was used for hyperparameter tuning or just held out, but it is part of the internal split.

testing.notes: The official test set from the ISIC archive, reduced from 1,512 images by excluding rare classes (VASC, DF, AKIEK). Used for final performance evaluation of all models.

pretraining_or_transfer_learning.notes: The paper states that the CNN models (GoogLeNet, InceptionV3, Xception, ResNet18, ResNet50, ResNet101, DenseNet201) were 'pre-trained' and used for transfer learning. While not explicitly named as ImageNet in the text, these standard architectures are pre-trained on ImageNet. The paper notes that Bazgir et al. trained from scratch, implying this study used pre-trained weights.

synthetic_or_augmented_data.notes: Synthetic data generated via image scaling (1 to 1.2), flipping (x/y-axis), and rotation (-90 to 90 degrees) to balance class distribution. This augmented dataset was used for training the feature extractors.

evaluation_protocol.split_details: The ISIC2018 dataset was originally divided into training (10,015), validation (193), and test (1,512) sets. After merging with ISIC2019 and excluding rare classes, the training pool became ISIC2018+ (16,787 images) and the test set became 1,390 images. The validation set count remained 193. No specific split percentage is given for the final training/test split other than the fixed ISIC archive splits.
evaluation_protocol.unseen_data_details: No unseen external dataset was used. The test set is the official ISIC2018 test set, which is held-out from the training pool but not from a different external cohort.

dataset_level_results.model_or_method: Seven CNNs (GoogLeNet, InceptionV3, Xception, ResNet18, ResNet50, ResNet101, DenseNet201) with Softmax

metrics.accuracy: Varied by model (e.g., DenseNet201: 85.90% Multi-Class Accuracy)

dataset_level_results.notes: Results for individual CNNs trained on the non-augmented combined dataset.

dataset_level_results.model_or_method: Top 3 CNNs (InceptionV3, Xception, DenseNet201) with Softmax

metrics.accuracy: Varied by model (e.g., InceptionV3: 86.55% Multi-Class Accuracy)

dataset_level_results.notes: Results for top-performing CNNs trained on the augmented dataset.

dataset_level_results.model_or_method: Proposed Ensemble Model (InceptionV3 + Xception + DenseNet201 features + SVM)
dataset_level_results.notes: The ensemble model outperformed individual CNNs and other ensemble methods like Max Voting (94.12%).

generalizability_notes: The dataset lacks diversity in skin tones and age groups, potentially introducing demographic biases.
The model was trained exclusively on dermoscopic images captured under controlled conditions; performance may degrade on real-world data with varying lighting/conditions.
Data augmentation artificially balanced classes (25% each), which may not reflect true clinical prevalence.
The study excludes rare but clinically significant lesion classes (VASC, DF, AKIEK), limiting applicability to the full spectrum of skin lesions.
No external validation on independent datasets outside the ISIC archive was performed, raising concerns about overfitting to the specific distribution of ISIC2018/2019.
Comparison with prior works is not strictly one-to-one due to differences in datasets (e.g., HAM10000 vs ISIC) and number of classes.
information_not_reported: Specific patient-level separation details (whether images from the same patient appear in both train and test sets).
Exact number of images per class in the final test set (1,390 total, but per-class breakdown not explicitly stated in results table, only percentages in augmented training set).
Specific hyperparameters for the SVM classifier beyond 'Lagrange Multipliers' and 'Convex Optimization'.
Statistical significance testing (p-values) for performance differences.
